content.text:
Take the spec @specs/lex-vietnamese-telex-input-method.md and the tests (may not cover all cases) @src/lex.zig as source of truth. Discuss with me the approach to handle 'Z', 'z' branch.

- Followng existing code pattern, technique, convention.

Come up with the approach, then consult the Oracle for another perspective, but prevent the Oracle from producing any example code. You should not write any code.
I will read the referenced spec and `src/lex.zig` to extract the intended `Z`/`z` behavior and current branch patterns, then ask Oracle for a code-free design critique.

result.output:
# Lex - Vietnamese Telex input method

The specification of the Lex program, a Vietnamese Telex input method.

## Vietnamese Telex Rules

### Literal input characters

- When any of the following characters start the word (when the word is empty), change to literal
  input from the new character position: `F`, `J`, `W`, `Z` (ignore cases), because these characters
  don't start a Vietnamese word. This rule is higher priority than diacritics and tones rules.
- When any of the following characters are added to the word, change to literal input from the new
  character position: `F`, `J`, `W` (ignore cases), because these characters don't appear in formal
  Vietnamese spelling. `Z` exists in Vietnamese word such as _Dzũng_, _Hồ Dzếnh_... This rule is
  lower priority than diacritics and tones rules.
- Literal input remains until the new word.
- When the user backspaces pass before the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.

### Diacritics

Diacritics let a character have many variant. We only focus on character diacritics in this section.
Tones have a separate section.

#### Empty

- Any literal alphabet character has empty diacritic by default.
- Only a specific number of characters have other diacritic and it must follow the rules for those
  characters.

#### Circumflex

- Valid for: `A` (Â), `E` (Ê), `O` (Ô) and their lower cases.

#### Breve

- Valid for: `A` (Ă) and its lower case.

#### Horn

- Valid for: `O` (Ơ), `U` (Ư) and their lower cases.

#### Stroke

- Valid for: `D` (Đ) and its lower case.

#### Apply rules

- Only apply diacritic when the immediate previous character doesn't have any diacritic (empty).
- _Circumflex_, _stroke_ are triggered when user inputs the same character (ignore case) with the
  existing character when that character is valid for the applying diacritic.
  - `Aa` -> `Â`, `Ee` -> `Ê`, `Oo` -> `Ô`.
  - `Dd` -> `Đ`.
- _Breve_, _horn_ are triggered when user input `W`, `w` after the character when that character is
  valid for the applying diacritic. Otherwise, treat `W`, `w` literally.
  - `Aw` -> `Ă`.
  - `Ow` -> `Ơ`, `Uw` -> `Ư`.
- The existing character keep its case, tone.
- Treat out of scope characters as literal.

#### Override rules

- New valid diacritic can override existing valid diacritic of the same base character.
  - `Ăa` -> `Â`, `Âw` -> `Ă`.
  - `Ôw` -> `Ơ`, `Ơo` -> `Ô`.
- Only `A`, `O` have override rules because they have more than one possible character diacritic.
- Keep tone untouched.
- The existing character keep its case, tone.
- Treat out of scope characters as literal.

#### Cancel rules

- The cancellation is triggered if and only if user inputs the character (ignore case) that would
  produce the existing diacritic for the same base character of the immediate previous character.
- The existing character keeps its case, tone, but the diacritic is removed.
  - Circumflex: `Âa` -> `Aa`, `Ốo` -> `Óo`... and so on for other cases of the same kind.
  - Breve: `Ăw` -> `Aw`.
  - Horn: `Ưw` -> `Uw`... and so on for other cases of the same kind.
  - Stroke: `Đd` -> `Dd`, `đD` -> `dD`.
- The new character is literal, keeps its case, no tone.
- Switch to literal from the new character position and the following characters. Literal input
  remains until the new word.
  - Existing `Â`, input `aa`, result `Aaa`.
- When the user backspaces pass before the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.

#### Auto-fill rules

- The auto-fill is only applied for `ươ` syllable immediately before the user input, when one of the
  character in the syllable is missing horn: `uơ` and `ưo`.
- Fill the missing _horn_ when the input is one of these character (ignore case): `C`, `I`, `M`,
  `N`, `P`, `T`, `U` and their lower cases.
  - Fill missing horn on `U`: `uơn` -> `ươn`.
  - Fill missing horn on `O`: `ưóp` -> `ướp`.
- The new input character is appended literally.
- Tone and case of existing characters are preserved on the original characters.

### Tones

Tones let a word have many variants, each variant has different meaning. Only the following
Vietnamese vowel could have tones: `A`, `E`, `I`, `O`, `U`, `Y` and their corresponding variants.
These vowel could take any tone.

#### Level (ngang)

- The default tone, it's the vowel without any tone mark, e.g. `A`, `Ê`, `Ơ`...and all other
  corresponding variants.
- If a vowel has tone mark other than level, use `Z`, `z` to reset the tone to level.

#### Rising (sắc)

- Example of rising tone: `Á`, `Ế`, `Ớ`...and all other corresponding variants.
- Use `S`, `s` to put the rising tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `As` -> `Á`, `Ês` -> `Ế`, `Ơs`
  -> `Ớ`.

#### Falling (huyền)

- Example of falling tone: `À`, `Ề`, `Ờ`...and all other corresponding variants.
- Use `F`, `f` to put the falling tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Af` -> `À`, `Êf` -> `Ề`, `Ơf`
  -> `Ờ`.

#### Dipping-rising (hỏi)

- Example of dipping-rising tone: `Ả`, `Ể`, `Ở`...and all other corresponding variants.
- Use `R`, `r` to put the dipping-rising tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Ar` -> `Ả`, `Êr` ->
  `Ể`, `Ơr` -> `Ở`.

#### Rising-glottalized (ngã)

- Example of rising-glottalized tone: `Ã`, `Ễ`, `Ỡ`...and all other corresponding variants.
- Use `X`, `x` to put the rising-glottalized tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Ax` -> `Ã`, `Êx`
  -> `Ễ`, `Ơx` -> `Ỡ`.

#### Falling-glottalized (nặng)

- Example of falling-glottalized tone: `Ạ`, `Ệ`, `Ợ`...and all other corresponding variants.
- Use `J`, `j` to put the falling-glottalized tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Aj` -> `Ạ`, `Êj`
  -> `Ệ`, `Ơj` -> `Ợ`.

#### Apply rules

- Only trigger apply tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
  character literally.
- Trigger apply tone when user inputs the trigger character for tones: `S`, `F`, `R`, `X`, `J` and
  their lower cases.
- _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
- If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels, append new character literally.
- If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and all of them don't have any tone (level), use _tone
  position rules_ to apply the tone for the word.
- If _tone position rules_ could not put tone to the pseudo-word, append new character literally.

#### Cancel rules

- Only trigger apply tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
  character literally.
- Trigger apply tone when user inputs the trigger character for tones: `S`, `F`, `R`, `X`, `J` and
  their lower cases.
- _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
- If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels, handle by _apply rules_.
- If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and has the tone (other than level) on vowels match with
  the triggered tone. Reset that tone to level. Append new character literally.
- If the new character has been appended literally, switch to literal from the new character
  position and the following characters. Literal input remains until the new word.
- When the user backspaces pass before the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.

#### Reset rules

- Only trigger reset tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
  character literally.
- Trigger reset tone when user inputs the trigger character for reset: `Z`, `z`.
- _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
- If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and has tone (other than level) in one of the vowels,
  change that tone to level, keep case and diacritic.
- If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels, append the new character literally and remain
  Vietnamese input to continue process Vietnamese input such as `dzu + x` -> `dzũ`.
- If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and all of them don't have any tone (level), append the
  new character literally and switch to literal from the new character position and the following
  characters. Literal input remains until the new word.
- When the user backspaces pass before the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.

#### Override rules

- Only trigger override tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
  character literally.
- Trigger apply tone when user inputs the trigger character for tones: `S`, `F`, `R`, `X`, `J` and
  their lower cases.
- _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
- If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels, handle by _apply rules_.
- If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and has the tone (other than level) on vowels different
  from the triggered tone, reset that tone to level, use _tone position rules_ to apply new tone for
  the word. The override tone is always successful.

#### Scan pseudo-word

- Scan pseudo-word is a process to get the information needed from the existing characters to put
  the tone in the correct position.
- The existing characters must not be empty.
- Iterate backward from the last existing character (the new input is the tone trigger character).
- If a vowel is found first, continue go backward until scanned the first consonant (singular) or no
  character to scan, then stop.
- If a consonant is found first, continue go backward until found the first vowel, then continue go
  backward until scanned the first consonant (singular) or no character to scan, then stop.
- After iterated, a context have been formed either (in the left to right order, not the reverse)
  `<consonants>`, `<vowels><consonants>`, `<consonant><vowels><consonants>`, `<vowels>` or
  `<consonant><vowels>`.

#### Tone position rules

The process of applying tone to a word go through many steps:

- Focus on the `<vowels>` in peusdo-word, it could be one vowel but could be multiple vowels, the
  tone will be applied on one of these vowels.
- Exceptions:
  - Word exactly `QU` (and their lower cases), could not put tone, this is a consonant only.
- If one vowel, put the tone on that vowel.
- If multiple vowels, follow these rules, priority from top down:
  - Vowels has `Ơ` (and its lower case), put on the rightmost `Ơ`.
  - Vowels has one any of `Ê`, `Â`, `Ô`, `Ă`, `Ư` (and their lower cases), put on the rightmost
    listed vowel found by the reverse scan.
  - Word start with `GI`, `QU` (and their lower cases) and has more vowels, put on next vowel, `I` /
    `U` count as part of the consonant.
  - Vowels exactly `OA`, `OE`, `OO`, `UY` (and their lower cases), put on first vowel.
  - Vowels start with `OA`, `OE`, `OO`, `UY` (and their lower cases) and has more vowels or
    consonants, put on second vowel.
  - Otherwise, put on first vowel.
- Preserve case and diacritic on existing vowels.
- Could not put tone for other cases.

content.text: I found `Z/z` is currently empty, while the spec and existing tests already distinguish it from `F/J/W`: initial `Z` starts literal, no-vowel pseudo-word appends `Z` but stays Telex, and toned vowel reset should not append the trigger. Next I will ask Oracle to critique the approach without providing code.

input.task: Review the intended approach for implementing the `Z`/`z` branch in a Vietnamese Telex input method. Do not produce any example code, pseudocode, snippets, or implementation text that resembles code. Provide only conceptual/behavioral critique and edge cases.
input.context: Source of truth: specs/lex-vietnamese-telex-input-method.md and tests in src/lex.zig. Current implementation has a State.add switch with branches for diacritics, W auto/literal behavior, and S rising tone branch; `Z`, `z` is currently empty. Existing helpers include pseudoword(), reset_tone(word), apply_tone(word,tone). Existing conventions: append_literal increments buffer_length and may set literal_index at the buffer_effective overflow boundary; every State.add action must decide buffer_modification_index; do not modify Span directly, replace via init* functions; `literal_index` gates literal mode. Need discuss approach only, no code writing.

1: # Lex - Vietnamese Telex input method
2: 
3: The specification of the Lex program, a Vietnamese Telex input method.
4: 
5: ## Vietnamese Telex Rules
6: 
7: ### Literal input characters
8: 
9: - When any of the following characters start the word (when the word is empty), change to literal
10:   input from the new character position: `F`, `J`, `W`, `Z` (ignore cases), because these characters
11:   don't start a Vietnamese word. This rule is higher priority than diacritics and tones rules.
12: - When any of the following characters are added to the word, change to literal input from the new
13:   character position: `F`, `J`, `W` (ignore cases), because these characters don't appear in formal
14:   Vietnamese spelling. `Z` exists in Vietnamese word such as _Dzũng_, _Hồ Dzếnh_... This rule is
15:   lower priority than diacritics and tones rules.
16: - Literal input remains until the new word.
17: - When the user backspaces pass before the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.
18: 
19: ### Diacritics
20: 
21: Diacritics let a character have many variant. We only focus on character diacritics in this section.
22: Tones have a separate section.
23: 
24: #### Empty
25: 
26: - Any literal alphabet character has empty diacritic by default.
27: - Only a specific number of characters have other diacritic and it must follow the rules for those
28:   characters.
29: 
30: #### Circumflex
31: 
32: - Valid for: `A` (Â), `E` (Ê), `O` (Ô) and their lower cases.
33: 
34: #### Breve
35: 
36: - Valid for: `A` (Ă) and its lower case.
37: 
38: #### Horn
39: 
40: - Valid for: `O` (Ơ), `U` (Ư) and their lower cases.
41: 
42: #### Stroke
43: 
44: - Valid for: `D` (Đ) and its lower case.
45: 
46: #### Apply rules
47: 
48: - Only apply diacritic when the immediate previous character doesn't have any diacritic (empty).
49: - _Circumflex_, _stroke_ are triggered when user inputs the same character (ignore case) with the
50:   existing character when that character is valid for the applying diacritic.
51:   - `Aa` -> `Â`, `Ee` -> `Ê`, `Oo` -> `Ô`.
52:   - `Dd` -> `Đ`.
53: - _Breve_, _horn_ are triggered when user input `W`, `w` after the character when that character is
54:   valid for the applying diacritic. Otherwise, treat `W`, `w` literally.
55:   - `Aw` -> `Ă`.
56:   - `Ow` -> `Ơ`, `Uw` -> `Ư`.
57: - The existing character keep its case, tone.
58: - Treat out of scope characters as literal.
59: 
60: #### Override rules
61: 
62: - New valid diacritic can override existing valid diacritic of the same base character.
63:   - `Ăa` -> `Â`, `Âw` -> `Ă`.
64:   - `Ôw` -> `Ơ`, `Ơo` -> `Ô`.
65: - Only `A`, `O` have override rules because they have more than one possible character diacritic.
66: - Keep tone untouched.
67: - The existing character keep its case, tone.
68: - Treat out of scope characters as literal.
69: 
70: #### Cancel rules
71: 
72: - The cancellation is triggered if and only if user inputs the character (ignore case) that would
73:   produce the existing diacritic for the same base character of the immediate previous character.
74: - The existing character keeps its case, tone, but the diacritic is removed.
75:   - Circumflex: `Âa` -> `Aa`, `Ốo` -> `Óo`... and so on for other cases of the same kind.
76:   - Breve: `Ăw` -> `Aw`.
77:   - Horn: `Ưw` -> `Uw`... and so on for other cases of the same kind.
78:   - Stroke: `Đd` -> `Dd`, `đD` -> `dD`.
79: - The new character is literal, keeps its case, no tone.
80: - Switch to literal from the new character position and the following characters. Literal input
81:   remains until the new word.
82:   - Existing `Â`, input `aa`, result `Aaa`.
83: - When the user backspaces pass before the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.
84: 
85: #### Auto-fill rules
86: 
87: - The auto-fill is only applied for `ươ` syllable immediately before the user input, when one of the
88:   character in the syllable is missing horn: `uơ` and `ưo`.
89: - Fill the missing _horn_ when the input is one of these character (ignore case): `C`, `I`, `M`,
90:   `N`, `P`, `T`, `U` and their lower cases.
91:   - Fill missing horn on `U`: `uơn` -> `ươn`.
92:   - Fill missing horn on `O`: `ưóp` -> `ướp`.
93: - The new input character is appended literally.
94: - Tone and case of existing characters are preserved on the original characters.
95: 
96: ### Tones
97: 
98: Tones let a word have many variants, each variant has different meaning. Only the following
99: Vietnamese vowel could have tones: `A`, `E`, `I`, `O`, `U`, `Y` and their corresponding variants.
100: These vowel could take any tone.
101: 
102: #### Level (ngang)
103: 
104: - The default tone, it's the vowel without any tone mark, e.g. `A`, `Ê`, `Ơ`...and all other
105:   corresponding variants.
106: - If a vowel has tone mark other than level, use `Z`, `z` to reset the tone to level.
107: 
108: #### Rising (sắc)
109: 
110: - Example of rising tone: `Á`, `Ế`, `Ớ`...and all other corresponding variants.
111: - Use `S`, `s` to put the rising tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `As` -> `Á`, `Ês` -> `Ế`, `Ơs`
112:   -> `Ớ`.
113: 
114: #### Falling (huyền)
115: 
116: - Example of falling tone: `À`, `Ề`, `Ờ`...and all other corresponding variants.
117: - Use `F`, `f` to put the falling tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Af` -> `À`, `Êf` -> `Ề`, `Ơf`
118:   -> `Ờ`.
119: 
120: #### Dipping-rising (hỏi)
121: 
122: - Example of dipping-rising tone: `Ả`, `Ể`, `Ở`...and all other corresponding variants.
123: - Use `R`, `r` to put the dipping-rising tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Ar` -> `Ả`, `Êr` ->
124:   `Ể`, `Ơr` -> `Ở`.
125: 
126: #### Rising-glottalized (ngã)
127: 
128: - Example of rising-glottalized tone: `Ã`, `Ễ`, `Ỡ`...and all other corresponding variants.
129: - Use `X`, `x` to put the rising-glottalized tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Ax` -> `Ã`, `Êx`
130:   -> `Ễ`, `Ơx` -> `Ỡ`.
131: 
132: #### Falling-glottalized (nặng)
133: 
134: - Example of falling-glottalized tone: `Ạ`, `Ệ`, `Ợ`...and all other corresponding variants.
135: - Use `J`, `j` to put the falling-glottalized tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Aj` -> `Ạ`, `Êj`
136:   -> `Ệ`, `Ơj` -> `Ợ`.
137: 
138: #### Apply rules
139: 
140: - Only trigger apply tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
141:   character literally.
142: - Trigger apply tone when user inputs the trigger character for tones: `S`, `F`, `R`, `X`, `J` and
143:   their lower cases.
144: - _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
145: - If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels, append new character literally.
146: - If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and all of them don't have any tone (level), use _tone
147:   position rules_ to apply the tone for the word.
148: - If _tone position rules_ could not put tone to the pseudo-word, append new character literally.
149: 
150: #### Cancel rules
151: 
152: - Only trigger apply tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
153:   character literally.
154: - Trigger apply tone when user inputs the trigger character for tones: `S`, `F`, `R`, `X`, `J` and
155:   their lower cases.
156: - _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
157: - If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels, handle by _apply rules_.
158: - If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and has the tone (other than level) on vowels match with
159:   the triggered tone. Reset that tone to level. Append new character literally.
160: - If the new character has been appended literally, switch to literal from the new character
161:   position and the following characters. Literal input remains until the new word.
162: - When the user backspaces pass before the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.
163: 
164: #### Reset rules
165: 
166: - Only trigger reset tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
167:   character literally.
168: - Trigger reset tone when user inputs the trigger character for reset: `Z`, `z`.
169: - _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
170: - If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and has tone (other than level) in one of the vowels,
171:   change that tone to level, keep case and diacritic.
172: - If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels, append the new character literally and remain
173:   Vietnamese input to continue process Vietnamese input such as `dzu + x` -> `dzũ`.
174: - If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and all of them don't have any tone (level), append the
175:   new character literally and switch to literal from the new character position and the following
176:   characters. Literal input remains until the new word.
177: - When the user backspaces pass before the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.
178: 
179: #### Override rules
180: 
181: - Only trigger override tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
182:   character literally.
183: - Trigger apply tone when user inputs the trigger character for tones: `S`, `F`, `R`, `X`, `J` and
184:   their lower cases.
185: - _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
186: - If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels, handle by _apply rules_.
187: - If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and has the tone (other than level) on vowels different
188:   from the triggered tone, reset that tone to level, use _tone position rules_ to apply new tone for
189:   the word. The override tone is always successful.
190: 
191: #### Scan pseudo-word
192: 
193: - Scan pseudo-word is a process to get the information needed from the existing characters to put
194:   the tone in the correct position.
195: - The existing characters must not be empty.
196: - Iterate backward from the last existing character (the new input is the tone trigger character).
197: - If a vowel is found first, continue go backward until scanned the first consonant (singular) or no
198:   character to scan, then stop.
199: - If a consonant is found first, continue go backward until found the first vowel, then continue go
200:   backward until scanned the first consonant (singular) or no character to scan, then stop.
201: - After iterated, a context have been formed either (in the left to right order, not the reverse)
202:   `<consonants>`, `<vowels><consonants>`, `<consonant><vowels><consonants>`, `<vowels>` or
203:   `<consonant><vowels>`.
204: 
205: #### Tone position rules
206: 
207: The process of applying tone to a word go through many steps:
208: 
209: - Focus on the `<vowels>` in peusdo-word, it could be one vowel but could be multiple vowels, the
210:   tone will be applied on one of these vowels.
211: - Exceptions:
212:   - Word exactly `QU` (and their lower cases), could not put tone, this is a consonant only.
213: - If one vowel, put the tone on that vowel.
214: - If multiple vowels, follow these rules, priority from top down:
215:   - Vowels has `Ơ` (and its lower case), put on the rightmost `Ơ`.
216:   - Vowels has one any of `Ê`, `Â`, `Ô`, `Ă`, `Ư` (and their lower cases), put on the rightmost
217:     listed vowel found by the reverse scan.
218:   - Word start with `GI`, `QU` (and their lower cases) and has more vowels, put on next vowel, `I` /
219:     `U` count as part of the consonant.
220:   - Vowels exactly `OA`, `OE`, `OO`, `UY` (and their lower cases), put on first vowel.
221:   - Vowels start with `OA`, `OE`, `OO`, `UY` (and their lower cases) and has more vowels or
222:     consonants, put on second vowel.
223:   - Otherwise, put on first vowel.
224: - Preserve case and diacritic on existing vowels.
225: - Could not put tone for other cases.

input.pattern: expect State.add handles .*S|expect State.add handles .*W|expect State.backspace|literal_index

result: /<redacted>/lex/src/lex.zig:286:// Should never set literal_index in .literal mode.
/<redacted>/lex/src/lex.zig:292:// When literal_index is set, mode must be .telex.
/<redacted>/lex/src/lex.zig:294:// The literal_index value must be in range null or 0 -> 16 (buffer_effective length)
/<redacted>/lex/src/lex.zig:297:// The literal_index value must be less than buffer length.
/<redacted>/lex/src/lex.zig:304:assert(self.buffer_length <= self.buffer_effective.len or self.mode == .literal or self.literal_index != null);
/<redacted>/lex/src/lex.zig:2478:// reset_tone followed by apply_tone for the override branch.
If you meant to search for a literal string, run the Grep tool again with literal:true.

Current implementation has a State.add switch with branches for diacritics, W auto/literal behavior, and S rising tone branch; `Z`, `z` is currently empty. Existing helpers include pseudoword(), reset_tone(word), apply_tone(word,tone). Existing conventions: append_literal increments buffer_length and may set literal_index at the buffer_effective overflow boundary; every State.add action must decide buffer_modification_index; do not modify Span directly, replace via init* functions; `literal_index` gates literal mode. Need discuss approach only, no code writing.

run.result:
1) TL;DR

The intended Z branch should be treated as a tone-reset branch, not as another tone-apply branch. The simple correct behavior is: start-of-word Z becomes literal immediately; otherwise inspect the current pseudo-word, consume Z only when there is an existing non-level tone to clear, keep Z literal-but-still-Telex when there are no vowels, and make Z literal-plus-literal-mode when vowels exist but are already level. Scope: S.

2) Recommended approach (simple path)

1. Keep the start-of-word rule separate and highest priority.
   - At the beginning of a word, Z should behave like the other non-Vietnamese starters and immediately start literal input from that character.
   - This is a lexical rule, not a tone rule, so it should happen before any pseudo-word analysis.

2. Reuse the pseudo-word scan, but do not reuse tone-placement logic.
   - For Z, the only question is whether the current pseudo-word already contains a non-level tone on one of its vowels.
   - If yes, clear that tone in place and consume the trigger.
   - Do not recompute tone position, because reset should undo the tone that actually exists, not re-decide where tone “would” belong.

3. Preserve the spec’s asymmetric middle-of-word behavior.
   - If the current pseudo-word has no vowels, append Z literally and stay in Vietnamese processing.
   - If the current pseudo-word has vowels and they are all level, append Z literally and switch to literal mode from that new Z onward.
   - This distinction is the most important behavioral difference from the other tone keys.

4. Treat successful reset as a pure edit, not a cancellation-with-literal.
   - When Z successfully resets an existing tone, the Z should disappear semantically; it should not be appended.
   - That makes Z meaningfully different from the same-tone cancellation behavior used by the tone keys such as S.

5. Be strict about bookkeeping on every exit path.
   - Successful reset should report the modified vowel position.
   - Every append-only path should explicitly leave the modification index empty, even if it was already cleared earlier in the function.
   - That keeps the branch aligned with the rest of the switch and avoids subtle synthetic-backspace bugs later.

3) Rationale and trade-offs

This is the simplest correct shape because it matches the spec directly and fits the current architecture:

- It uses the existing pseudo-word concept instead of inventing new scanning rules.
- It uses the existing tone-reset helper, which already preserves case and character diacritic.
- It avoids overfitting Z into the same mental model as the tone-application branches.

The main alternative would be to generalize all tone-trigger handling into one shared flow now, but that is unnecessary for a single branch and adds risk before the remaining tone branches are finished.

4) Risks and guardrails

- Biggest behavioral trap: treating Z like “level-tone cancellation.”
  - That would be wrong because successful Z reset should not append the trigger.
  - Same-tone cancellation for the other tone keys does append the trigger and enters literal mode; Z does not do that on success.

- Biggest state trap: accidentally entering literal mode on the no-vowel path.
  - The spec explicitly wants continued Vietnamese processing there so names/forms like the Dz case still work with later tone input.
  - The only exception is the global buffer-overflow behavior from the literal-append helper; that should remain a separate guardrail, not be overridden locally.

- Biggest bookkeeping trap: forgetting to make an explicit modification-index decision on the append-only branch where vowels exist but tone is already level.
  - Behavior may still appear correct because the index is cleared at function entry, but it becomes inconsistent with the stated branch discipline.

- Tone reset should preserve everything except tone.
  - Case must remain unchanged.
  - Character diacritic must remain unchanged.
  - Only the tone mark is removed.

- Do not add special placement exceptions such as QU or GI into the reset-success path.
  - Those matter when deciding where to place a tone.
  - They do not matter when removing a tone that is already present.

- Internal invariant: there should be at most one non-level tone in the vowel run.
  - Asserting this is consistent with the rest of the implementation style.
  - If you ever expect malformed seeded states in tests, decide explicitly whether assertions are acceptable there.

Key edge cases worth validating conceptually:
- start-of-word Z
- middle-of-word Z after only consonants
- middle-of-word Z after a toned vowel
- middle-of-word Z after vowels that are already level
- mixed case on both trigger and target vowel
- vowels with diacritics already present
- multi-vowel pseudo-words where the tone is not on the first vowel
- suffix-only pseudo-word behavior when earlier characters exist in the buffer
- backspace crossing the literal point after the level-vowel path
- buffer-overflow boundary during literal append

5) When to consider the advanced path

Only revisit with a more abstract design if:
- you are implementing the remaining tone branches next,
- branch duplication starts causing inconsistent literal-mode behavior,
- or synthetic-backspace bookkeeping becomes hard to reason about across tone triggers.

6) Optional advanced path

A reasonable later refactor is a shared “tone trigger orchestrator” that handles:
- pseudo-word discovery,
- literal gating,
- existing-tone discovery,
- and consistent modification-index decisions,

while leaving each trigger to define only its policy:
- apply,
- override,
- cancel,
- or reset.

That is probably worth doing only after Z and at least one or two more tone branches are complete, so the common shape is real rather than guessed.

result.content:
1: # Lex - Vietnamese Telex input method
2: 
3: The specification of the Lex program, a Vietnamese Telex input method.
4: 
5: ## Vietnamese Telex Rules
6: 
7: ### Literal input characters
8: 
9: - When any of the following characters start the word (when the word is empty), change to literal
10:   input from the new character position: `F`, `J`, `W`, `Z` (ignore cases), because these characters
11:   don't start a Vietnamese word. This rule is higher priority than diacritics and tones rules.
12: - When any of the following characters are added to the word, change to literal input from the new
13:   character position: `F`, `J`, `W` (ignore cases), because these characters don't appear in formal
14:   Vietnamese spelling. `Z` exists in Vietnamese word such as _Dzũng_, _Hồ Dzếnh_... This rule is
15:   lower priority than diacritics and tones rules.
16: - Literal input remains until the new word.
17: - When the user backspaces pass before the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.
18: 
19: ### Diacritics
20: 
21: Diacritics let a character have many variant. We only focus on character diacritics in this section.
22: Tones have a separate section.
23: 
24: #### Empty
25: 
26: - Any literal alphabet character has empty diacritic by default.
27: - Only a specific number of characters have other diacritic and it must follow the rules for those
28:   characters.
29: 
30: #### Circumflex
31: 
32: - Valid for: `A` (Â), `E` (Ê), `O` (Ô) and their lower cases.
33: 
34: #### Breve
35: 
36: - Valid for: `A` (Ă) and its lower case.
37: 
38: #### Horn
39: 
40: - Valid for: `O` (Ơ), `U` (Ư) and their lower cases.
41: 
42: #### Stroke
43: 
44: - Valid for: `D` (Đ) and its lower case.
45: 
46: #### Apply rules
47: 
48: - Only apply diacritic when the immediate previous character doesn't have any diacritic (empty).
49: - _Circumflex_, _stroke_ are triggered when user inputs the same character (ignore case) with the
50:   existing character when that character is valid for the applying diacritic.
51:   - `Aa` -> `Â`, `Ee` -> `Ê`, `Oo` -> `Ô`.
52:   - `Dd` -> `Đ`.
53: - _Breve_, _horn_ are triggered when user input `W`, `w` after the character when that character is
54:   valid for the applying diacritic. Otherwise, treat `W`, `w` literally.
55:   - `Aw` -> `Ă`.
56:   - `Ow` -> `Ơ`, `Uw` -> `Ư`.
57: - The existing character keep its case, tone.
58: - Treat out of scope characters as literal.
59: 
60: #### Override rules
61: 
62: - New valid diacritic can override existing valid diacritic of the same base character.
63:   - `Ăa` -> `Â`, `Âw` -> `Ă`.
64:   - `Ôw` -> `Ơ`, `Ơo` -> `Ô`.
65: - Only `A`, `O` have override rules because they have more than one possible character diacritic.
66: - Keep tone untouched.
67: - The existing character keep its case, tone.
68: - Treat out of scope characters as literal.
69: 
70: #### Cancel rules
71: 
72: - The cancellation is triggered if and only if user inputs the character (ignore case) that would
73:   produce the existing diacritic for the same base character of the immediate previous character.
74: - The existing character keeps its case, tone, but the diacritic is removed.
75:   - Circumflex: `Âa` -> `Aa`, `Ốo` -> `Óo`... and so on for other cases of the same kind.
76:   - Breve: `Ăw` -> `Aw`.
77:   - Horn: `Ưw` -> `Uw`... and so on for other cases of the same kind.
78:   - Stroke: `Đd` -> `Dd`, `đD` -> `dD`.
79: - The new character is literal, keeps its case, no tone.
80: - Switch to literal from the new character position and the following characters. Literal input
81:   remains until the new word.
82:   - Existing `Â`, input `aa`, result `Aaa`.
83: - When the user backspaces pass before the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.
84: 
85: #### Auto-fill rules
86: 
87: - The auto-fill is only applied for `ươ` syllable immediately before the user input, when one of the
88:   character in the syllable is missing horn: `uơ` and `ưo`.
89: - Fill the missing _horn_ when the input is one of these character (ignore case): `C`, `I`, `M`,
90:   `N`, `P`, `T`, `U` and their lower cases.
91:   - Fill missing horn on `U`: `uơn` -> `ươn`.
92:   - Fill missing horn on `O`: `ưóp` -> `ướp`.
93: - The new input character is appended literally.
94: - Tone and case of existing characters are preserved on the original characters.
95: 
96: ### Tones
97: 
98: Tones let a word have many variants, each variant has different meaning. Only the following
99: Vietnamese vowel could have tones: `A`, `E`, `I`, `O`, `U`, `Y` and their corresponding variants.
100: These vowel could take any tone.
101: 
102: #### Level (ngang)
103: 
104: - The default tone, it's the vowel without any tone mark, e.g. `A`, `Ê`, `Ơ`...and all other
105:   corresponding variants.
106: - If a vowel has tone mark other than level, use `Z`, `z` to reset the tone to level.
107: 
108: #### Rising (sắc)
109: 
110: - Example of rising tone: `Á`, `Ế`, `Ớ`...and all other corresponding variants.
111: - Use `S`, `s` to put the rising tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `As` -> `Á`, `Ês` -> `Ế`, `Ơs`
112:   -> `Ớ`.
113: 
114: #### Falling (huyền)
115: 
116: - Example of falling tone: `À`, `Ề`, `Ờ`...and all other corresponding variants.
117: - Use `F`, `f` to put the falling tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Af` -> `À`, `Êf` -> `Ề`, `Ơf`
118:   -> `Ờ`.
119: 
120: #### Dipping-rising (hỏi)
121: 
122: - Example of dipping-rising tone: `Ả`, `Ể`, `Ở`...and all other corresponding variants.
123: - Use `R`, `r` to put the dipping-rising tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Ar` -> `Ả`, `Êr` ->
124:   `Ể`, `Ơr` -> `Ở`.
125: 
126: #### Rising-glottalized (ngã)
127: 
128: - Example of rising-glottalized tone: `Ã`, `Ễ`, `Ỡ`...and all other corresponding variants.
129: - Use `X`, `x` to put the rising-glottalized tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Ax` -> `Ã`, `Êx`
130:   -> `Ễ`, `Ơx` -> `Ỡ`.
131: 
132: #### Falling-glottalized (nặng)
133: 
134: - Example of falling-glottalized tone: `Ạ`, `Ệ`, `Ợ`...and all other corresponding variants.
135: - Use `J`, `j` to put the falling-glottalized tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Aj` -> `Ạ`, `Êj`
136:   -> `Ệ`, `Ơj` -> `Ợ`.
137: 
138: #### Apply rules
139: 
140: - Only trigger apply tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
141:   character literally.
142: - Trigger apply tone when user inputs the trigger character for tones: `S`, `F`, `R`, `X`, `J` and
143:   their lower cases.
144: - _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
145: - If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels, append new character literally.
146: - If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and all of them don't have any tone (level), use _tone
147:   position rules_ to apply the tone for the word.
148: - If _tone position rules_ could not put tone to the pseudo-word, append new character literally.
149: 
150: #### Cancel rules
151: 
152: - Only trigger apply tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
153:   character literally.
154: - Trigger apply tone when user inputs the trigger character for tones: `S`, `F`, `R`, `X`, `J` and
155:   their lower cases.
156: - _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
157: - If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels, handle by _apply rules_.
158: - If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and has the tone (other than level) on vowels match with
159:   the triggered tone. Reset that tone to level. Append new character literally.
160: - If the new character has been appended literally, switch to literal from the new character
161:   position and the following characters. Literal input remains until the new word.
162: - When the user backspaces pass before the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.
163: 
164: #### Reset rules
165: 
166: - Only trigger reset tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
167:   character literally.
168: - Trigger reset tone when user inputs the trigger character for reset: `Z`, `z`.
169: - _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
170: - If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and has tone (other than level) in one of the vowels,
171:   change that tone to level, keep case and diacritic.
172: - If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels, append the new character literally and remain
173:   Vietnamese input to continue process Vietnamese input such as `dzu + x` -> `dzũ`.
174: - If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and all of them don't have any tone (level), append the
175:   new character literally and switch to literal from the new character position and the following
176:   characters. Literal input remains until the new word.
177: - When the user backspaces pass before the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.
178: 
179: #### Override rules
180: 
181: - Only trigger override tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
182:   character literally.
183: - Trigger apply tone when user inputs the trigger character for tones: `S`, `F`, `R`, `X`, `J` and
184:   their lower cases.
185: - _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
186: - If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels, handle by _apply rules_.
187: - If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and has the tone (other than level) on vowels different
188:   from the triggered tone, reset that tone to level, use _tone position rules_ to apply new tone for
189:   the word. The override tone is always successful.
190: 
191: #### Scan pseudo-word
192: 
193: - Scan pseudo-word is a process to get the information needed from the existing characters to put
194:   the tone in the correct position.
195: - The existing characters must not be empty.
196: - Iterate backward from the last existing character (the new input is the tone trigger character).
197: - If a vowel is found first, continue go backward until scanned the first consonant (singular) or no
198:   character to scan, then stop.
199: - If a consonant is found first, continue go backward until found the first vowel, then continue go
200:   backward until scanned the first consonant (singular) or no character to scan, then stop.
201: - After iterated, a context have been formed either (in the left to right order, not the reverse)
202:   `<consonants>`, `<vowels><consonants>`, `<consonant><vowels><consonants>`, `<vowels>` or
203:   `<consonant><vowels>`.
204: 
205: #### Tone position rules
206: 
207: The process of applying tone to a word go through many steps:
208: 
209: - Focus on the `<vowels>` in peusdo-word, it could be one vowel but could be multiple vowels, the
210:   tone will be applied on one of these vowels.
211: - Exceptions:
212:   - Word exactly `QU` (and their lower cases), could not put tone, this is a consonant only.
213: - If one vowel, put the tone on that vowel.
214: - If multiple vowels, follow these rules, priority from top down:
215:   - Vowels has `Ơ` (and its lower case), put on the rightmost `Ơ`.
216:   - Vowels has one any of `Ê`, `Â`, `Ô`, `Ă`, `Ư` (and their lower cases), put on the rightmost
217:     listed vowel found by the reverse scan.
218:   - Word start with `GI`, `QU` (and their lower cases) and has more vowels, put on next vowel, `I` /
219:     `U` count as part of the consonant.
220:   - Vowels exactly `OA`, `OE`, `OO`, `UY` (and their lower cases), put on first vowel.
221:   - Vowels start with `OA`, `OE`, `OO`, `UY` (and their lower cases) and has more vowels or
222:     consonants, put on second vowel.
223:   - Otherwise, put on first vowel.
224: - Preserve case and diacritic on existing vowels.
225: - Could not put tone for other cases.

result: /<redacted>/lex/src/lex.zig:1214:fn reset_tone(self: *State, word: Pseudoword, tone_index: u8) void {
/<redacted>/lex/src/lex.zig:2073:test "expect State.add switch to literal input when append F, J, W, Z (ignore cases) on empty buffer" {
/<redacted>/lex/src/lex.zig:2239:test "expect State.add appends Z literally and stays Vietnamese when pseudo-word has no vowel" {
/<redacted>/lex/src/lex.zig:2873:test "expect State can be initialized by raw allocation" {

input.pattern: Z literally|reset tone|literal_index.*Z|backspaces.*literal|stays Vietnamese|switch to literal input.*Z|tone.*Z

input.goal: Extract the intended implementation approach and any concerns already discussed for the Z/z reset-tone branch in src/lex.zig, especially how it should mirror existing State.add conventions, literal_index behavior, buffer_modification_index handling, and edge cases from the spec.

tool_uses.result:
### Intended Implementation Approach for 'Z' / 'z' Branch

Based on the specification and existing patterns in `src/lex.zig`, the handling of the `Z` and `z` characters should follow these logic paths within the `State.add` switch:

#### 1. Initial Word or Literal Mode
- **Condition**: If `self.literal_index != null`, `self.mode == .literal`, or `self.buffer_length == 0`.
- **Behavior**: Call `self.append_literal(c)`. 
- **Literal Index**: If the word is starting (`buffer_length == 0`), `self.literal_index` must be set to `0` because `Z` does not start formal Vietnamese words. This mirrors the behavior for `F`, `J`, and `W`.

#### 2. Tone Reset (Successful Reset)
- **Condition**: The `pseudoword()` has at least one vowel, and at least one of those vowels has a tone other than `.level`.
- **Behavior**: 
    - Call `self.reset_tone(word)` to change the tone to `.level` while preserving case and diacritics.
    - **Do not** append the `Z` character literally.
    - **Buffer Modification**: `self.buffer_modification_index` should be set to the earliest index modified by `reset_tone` (this is already handled inside the `reset_tone` function in the provided source).

#### 3. Literal Append (No Vowels)
- **Condition**: The `pseudoword()` has no vowels (e.g., the user is typing a name like "Dzũng").
- **Behavior**: 
    - Call `self.append_literal(c)`.
    - **Telex Continuity**: Unlike other literal appends, do **not** set `literal_index`. The spec explicitly states: "remain Vietnamese input to continue process Vietnamese input such as `dzu + x` -> `dzũ`."
    - **Buffer Modification**: Set `self.buffer_modification_index = null` as no existing spans were modified.

#### 4. Literal Append and Switch to Literal Mode (Vowels are Level)
- **Condition**: The `pseudoword()` has at least one vowel, but all vowels are already at `.level` tone.
- **Behavior**: 
    - Call `self.append_literal(c)`.
    - **Literal Index**: Set `self.literal_index = self.buffer_length - 1` (the position of the new `Z`). This triggers the rule: "switch to literal from the new character position and the following characters."
    - **Buffer Modification**: Set `self.buffer_modification_index = null`.

### Key Conventions and Concerns
- **Span Immutability**: Spans should not be modified directly. Use `Span.init_diacritic_tone` or the `reset_tone` helper to replace spans in the `buffer_effective` array.
- **Buffer Modification Index**: This must always be updated to the earliest modified index to ensure synthetic backspaces are calculated correctly by the caller.
- **Literal Index Gating**: The `literal_index` is the primary mechanism for disabling Telex processing for the remainder of the word. The `Z` branch is unique because it can append a character literally *without* setting the `literal_index` (in the "no vowels" case).
- **Pseudoword Scanning**: The `pseudoword()` helper should be used to identify the vowel range and word boundaries before deciding which sub-branch to execute.

The simple correct behavior is: start-of-word Z becomes literal immediately; otherwise inspect the current pseudo-word, consume Z only when there is an existing non-level tone to clear, keep Z literal-but-still-Telex when there are no vowels, and make Z literal-plus-literal-mode when vowels exist but are already level. 2) Recommended approach (simple path)

1. Keep the start-of-word rule separate and highest priority.
   - At the beginning of a word, Z should behave like the other non-Vietnamese starters and immediately start literal input from that character.

This is the simplest correct shape because it matches the spec directly and fits the current architecture:

- It uses the existing pseudo-word concept instead of inventing new scanning rules.
- It uses the existing tone-reset helper, which already preserves case and character diacritic.

The main alternative would be to generalize all tone-trigger handling into one shared flow now, but that is unnecessary for a single branch and adds risk before the remaining tone branches are finished.

- start-of-word Z

5) When to consider the advanced path

Only revisit with a more abstract design if:
- you are implementing the remaining tone branches next,
- branch duplication starts causing inconsistent literal-mode behavior,
- or synthetic-backspace bookkeeping becomes hard to reason about across tone triggers.

6) Optional advanced path

A reasonable later refactor is a shared “tone trigger orchestrator” that handles:
- pseudo-word discovery,

while leaving each trigger to define only its policy:

content.text:
Oracle’s main critique matches the spec: treat `Z/z` as a tone-reset trigger with special literal behavior, not as another normal tone key.

## Recommended `Z/z` branch approach

Current state: the `Z/z` arm is empty in [`State.add`](file:///<redacted>/lex/src/lex.zig#L918), while related patterns already exist in the `S/s` tone branch and literal branches.

### 1. Top priority: respect existing literal gates

For `Z/z`, first handle the same high-level gates as other branches:

- If `literal_index` is already set: append `Z/z` literally; do not process Telex.
- If `mode` is `.literal`: append literally; do not set `literal_index`.
- If `buffer_length == 0` in Telex mode: append `Z/z` literally and set `literal_index` to the new character position.

That last case is required by the spec’s “word starts with `F`, `J`, `W`, `Z`” rule and is already tested in [`expect State.add switch to literal input when append F, J, W, Z ... on empty buffer`](file:///<redacted>/lex/src/lex.zig#L2020-L2043).

Important detail: avoid blindly copying the `F/J/W` combined guard if it would set `literal_index` while `mode == .literal`, because the invariant says literal mode must not have `literal_index`.

### 2. For non-empty Telex input, scan the pseudo-word

Use the existing `pseudoword()` helper, like the `S/s` branch does around [`State.add`](file:///<redacted>/lex/src/lex.zig#L720-L767).

Then split by the reset rules:

#### A. Pseudo-word has no vowels

Append `Z/z` literally.

Do **not** set `literal_index`, except for the automatic overflow behavior inside `append_literal`.

This is the distinct `Z` behavior from the spec: no-vowel `Z` remains Vietnamese input so later Vietnamese processing can still happen. This is already covered by [`expect State.add appends Z literally and stays Vietnamese when pseudo-word has no vowel`](file:///<redacted>/lex/src/lex.zig#L2185-L2265).

#### B. Pseudo-word has vowels and at least one non-level tone

Reset the existing tone to `.level`.

Do **not** append `Z/z`.

Do **not** enter literal mode.

Use the existing `reset_tone(word)` helper because it already preserves base case and diacritic while replacing the span through `Span.init_diacritic_tone`, matching the architectural rule not to mutate `Span` directly.

Bookkeeping:

- `buffer_length` unchanged.
- `literal_index` unchanged, normally `null`.
- `buffer_modification_index` becomes the toned vowel index.

This differs from `S/s` cancellation: `S/s` cancellation resets tone, appends literal `s`, and enters literal mode; `Z/z` successful reset only resets tone and consumes the trigger.

#### C. Pseudo-word has vowels, but all are level

Append `Z/z` literally and switch to literal input from that new character position.

Bookkeeping should mirror cancellation-style branches:

- Existing spans unchanged.
- `buffer_modification_index = null`.
- `literal_index` points to the appended `Z/z` position.
- `buffer_length` increases by one.

This is the “level vowel + Z means literal Z and following literal text” case.

### 3. Tone discovery should follow existing `S/s` technique

The current `S/s` branch scans the vowel range and asserts that there is at most one non-level tone before deciding apply / override / cancel.

For `Z/z`, use the same conceptual scan:

- inspect only `word.vowels_start` through `word.vowels_end`;
- find whether a vowel already has non-level tone;
- assert no malformed multiple-tone pseudo-word if following existing internal-invariant style.

Then call `reset_tone(word)` only for the successful-reset path.

Do not use `apply_tone`. `Z` has no tone-position work.

### 4. Do not add `QU` / `GI` placement exceptions to `Z`

Oracle specifically called this out, and I agree.

`QU`, `GI`, `OA`, `UY`, etc. are tone-placement concerns. `Z` only removes an existing tone if one exists. If no tone exists, the reset rule is governed by whether the pseudo-word has vowels and whether they are level.

So for `Z`:

- no special tone-position rules;
- no recomputation of where the tone “should” be;
- reset the tone that actually exists.

### 5. Tests likely missing before implementation

Existing tests already cover:

- start-of-word `Z/z` starts literal;
- no-vowel pseudo-word appends `Z/z` and stays Telex.

Likely missing cases to add before or with implementation:

1. `Z/z` resets existing tone and is consumed.
2. Reset preserves base case.
3. Reset preserves diacritic.
4. Reset works in multi-vowel pseudo-words when tone is not on the first vowel.
5. Vowels with all level tones append `Z/z` and set `literal_index`.
6. Backspace over that appended literal `Z/z` clears `literal_index`.
7. Boundary case where appending literal `Z/z` crosses `buffer_effective.len`.
8. Suffix pseudo-word behavior: only the trailing pseudo-word should be inspected, same as tone branches.

## Final approach

Implement `Z/z` as a small, local branch following the existing `S/s` tone-scan shape but with reset-only policy:

1. literal/empty guards;
2. `pseudoword()`;
3. no vowels → append literal, stay Telex;
4. vowels with non-level tone → `reset_tone`, consume `Z/z`;
5. vowels all level → append literal and set `literal_index`.

No new abstraction is needed yet. A shared tone-trigger orchestrator can wait until more tone branches are implemented.
